coredata.dc:title: Tuning photoluminescence of graphene oxide quantum dots from yellow to cyan

Cheng, Longxu
Luo, Hongyi
Ouyang, Hong

Yang, Yong
Zhang, Qiwei
Kawazoe, Yoshiyuki
coredata.dc:description: Cyan photoluminescent materials have a wide range of applications in many fields. In this work, boron-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (BGOQDs) were synthesized through a thermal annealing method, and their microstructures and optical properties were studied. The results showed that compared to GOQDs, a yellow to cyan tunable photoluminescence (PL) of BGOQDs can be obtained by regulating the annealing temperature. The PL emission peaks of BGOQDs have a maximal blue-shift of about 35.5 nm compared to that of the GOQDs. Density functional theory(DFT) calculations demonstrate that the observed PL blue-shift in BGOQDs may be understood as follows: The increase of B-C bonds introduces holes to the electron bonding states especially the state related to the valence band maximum (VBM), which lowers the position of VBM and enlarges the band gap and then leads to a PL blue-shift.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cyan photoluminescence
Blue-shift
Boron-doped graphene oxide quantum dots
DFT calculations

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region (No.2021GXNSFAA220044), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12164005, 11664003, 11474285, 12104106) P. R. China. We thank the staff of the Center for Computational Materials Science, Institute for Materials Research, Tohoku University, and the supercomputer resources through the HPCI System Research Project (hp200246). The authors also appreciate the staff of Hefei Advanced Computing Center for the support of supercomputing facilities. One of the authors (Y. Kawazoe), is supported by Suranaree University of Technology (SUT), Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI), and National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF). The supplementary material provides the high-resolution O1s spectra for GOQDs and BGOQDs.

This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region (No.2021GXNSFAA220044), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12164005, 11664003, 11474285, 12104106) P. R. China. We thank the staff of the Center for Computational Materials Science, Institute for Materials Research, Tohoku University, and the supercomputer resources through the HPCI System Research Project (hp200246). The authors also appreciate the staff of Hefei Advanced Computing Center for the support of supercomputing facilities. One of the authors (Y. Kawazoe), is supported by Suranaree University of Technology (SUT), Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI), and National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) GOQDs and (b) BGOQDs360. Particle size distribution of (c) GOQDs and (d) BGOQDs360.
(a) Raman spectra and (b) FT-IR spectra of GOQDs and BGOQDs.

(a) The survey XPS spectra of GOQDs and BGOQDs. (b) A magnified view of the B1s region from the survey XPS spectrum. (c) XPS high-resolution C1s spectra of GOQDs and BGOQDs, (d) XPS high-resolution B1s spectra of BGOQDs.
PL spectra of each sample under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Normalized spectra of GOQDs and BGOQDs under 360 nm excitation. (b) UV–visible absorption spectra for GOQDs and BGOQDs. (c) Relationship between the PL blue-shift and the relative content of C-B of BGOQDs. (d) optical photographs of GOQDs and BGOQDs dispersed in water under 365 nm UV excitation.

Atomic models for the simulation of GOQD (a) and (b)-(c) configuration A (cfg. A) and configuration B (cfg. B) of BGOQDs, which are optimized by DFT calculations. (d) Calculated optical absorption spectra of BGOQDs.

Variation of the energy levels of BGOQDs (cfgs. A and B) related to the optical absorption/emission peaks at ∼ 2 eV (Fig. 6d). The electron densities (
ψ
Γ
VBM
2
,
ψ
Γ
CB
2
) of the related VBM and CB are shown along with the energy levels for the wave functions at the Γ point.
Elemental relative content of each sample obtained by XPS.
Relative proportions of C–C, C–O–C, CC, CO, O–CO, C–B, and C–O–B in each sample from C 1 s spectral analysis.

head.ce:title: Tuning photoluminescence of graphene oxide quantum dots from yellow to cyan

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cyan photoluminescent materials have a wide range of applications in many fields. In this work, boron-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (BGOQDs) were synthesized through a thermal annealing method, and their microstructures and optical properties were studied. The results showed that compared to GOQDs, a yellow to cyan tunable photoluminescence (PL) of BGOQDs can be obtained by regulating the annealing temperature. The PL emission peaks of BGOQDs have a maximal blue-shift of about 35.5 nm compared to that of the GOQDs. Density functional theory(DFT) calculations demonstrate that the observed PL blue-shift in BGOQDs may be understood as follows: The increase of B-C bonds introduces holes to the electron bonding states especially the state related to the valence band maximum (VBM), which lowers the position of VBM and enlarges the band gap and then leads to a PL blue-shift.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cyan photoluminescent nanomaterials have recently garnered significant attention due to their wide applications in the field of visible spectrum, such as biomedicine [1,2], environmental detection [3–5], lighting [6], displays and optical communications [3]. In recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have become a hot topic of research due to their exceptional optical properties, stability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and wide-range applications [7–9]. After years of extensive research, remarkable progress has been achieved in the development of highly luminescent GQDs. Notably, various GQDs with emission wavelengths in the range of ultraviolet [10], blue [11], green [12], yellow [13], and red [14] have been developed by different methods. Nevertheless, there is yet little research on the cyan fluorescent of GQDs.

Researches have demonstrated that heteroatom doping is an effective strategy to regulate the photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs. Boron (B) atoms have a lower electronegativity (2.04 eV) than that of carbon (C) atoms (2.55 eV), and the atomic size of B is quite similar to that of C, B doping can significantly alter the intrinsic properties of GQDs [15,16]. As a result, B doping has been utilized to modify the optics and other properties of carbon-based nanomaterials. For example, Zhang et al. [17] synthesized boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) by pyrolyzing a mixture of citric acid, histidine, serine, ethylenediamine, and boric acid. The BGQDs excited excitation-dependent fluorescence and were used as multicolor probe for detecting oxytetracycline in food and for information encryption. Hsu et al. [18] synthesized red-emitting BGQDs by a hydrothermal method derived from 1,3,6-Trinitropyrene and boric acid, resulting in a 7 nm red-shift in the PL emission peaks and improved luminescence intensity compared to that of undoped GQDs. Dey et al. [19] synthesized boron-doped graphene sheets via graphite arc discharge, then cut them into BGQDs. The BGQDs had a significant red-shift in the PL emission, which can be attributed to the formation of trap states in the π-π* gap. Similarly, Zhang et al. [20] employed a hydrothermal method to cut boron-doped graphene into BGQDs and used them as photoluminescent probes for label-free glucose sensing, observing a blue-shift of approximately 5 nm compared with B-free GQDs. Based on the above references, one can find that BGQDs with different PL can be obtained by different methods. However, as far as we know, there are few works reporting on how to obtain the GQDs with cyan PL. In addition, the related mechanism for the PL regulation of the BGQDs is far from clear. Consequently, it is urgent to develop an effective method to tuning the PL of GQDs to cyan light to widen its applicable range, and study its luminescence mechanism more systematically.

In this work, we present a strategy for preparing boron-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (BGOQDs) with tunable PL by a thermal annealing method, using GOQDs powder as the precursor and B₂O₃ as the dopant source. The obtained BGOQDs360 possess bright cyan luminescence when excited by a UV lamp at 365 nm. Furthermore, The PL emission of the BGOQDs exhibits a maximal blue-shift of approximately 35.5 nm compared to that of GOQDs. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations show that the increase of B-C bonds is equivalent to the introduction of holes to the electron bonding states especially the valence band maximum (VBM), which lowers the position of VBM and enlarges the band gap of BGOQDs, and then leads to a PL blue-shift in the BGOQDs. Thus, this study opens up a new way for preparing BGOQDs with cyan emission and presents an understanding for the mechanism of the observed colour tunability of BGOQDs.

Boric anhydride(B2O3:98 %) and nitric acid (HNO3:65–68 %) were purchased from XILONG SCIENTIFIC (Guangdong China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Vulcan XC-72 carbon black was purchased from Cabot Corporation (Boston, MA, USA). Deionized water was used in the experiment.

GOQDs were synthesized following a previously reported method [21]. The mixture of GOQDs powder (0.1 g) and B₂O₃ (1.0 g) was mixed uniformly and then was put in a small crucible, and was placed inside a quartz tube. With the quartz tube located in a horizontal reaction tube furnace (equipped with temperature and gas-flow control), the mixture was annealed in a flowing Ar gas (50 sccm) at 300–360 °C at a rate of 5 °C min−1 for 2 hours at atmospheric pressure. After the tube furnace cooling to room temperature, the obtained mixture was dissolved in water, then was filtered through a 220 nm filter membrane to remove large insoluble particles. After that, the mixture was futher purified by dialysis for 24 h using a dialysis membrane with 2000 MWCO, and the BGOQDs (BGOQDs300, BGOQDs320, BGOQDs340, and BGOQDs360, numeric numbers denote the annealing temperature) were obtained.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo SCIENTIFIC Nexsa instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected on a Brucker TENSOR 27 spectrometer. The morphologies of the samples were detected by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 F) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. PL measurements are performed on an RF-5301 PC PL spectrometer. Optical absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2700 spectrophotometer.

The first-principles calculations were carried out using VASP [22,23], a program which is based on DFT. The wave functions of electrons were expanded using a plane wave basis set with an energy cutoff of 600 eV, and the electron-ion interactions were described by the PAW potentials [24,25]. The PBE type functional was adopted to account for the exchange-correlation interactions of electrons [26]. For the total energy calculations of the BGOQD systems, an 8 × 8 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-points mesh [27] is generated for sampling the Brillouin zone (BZ).

The original GQDs are modeled by the C atoms around the geometric center of a (5 ×5) supercell of graphene sheet (hexagonal cell with the dimensional lengths of ∼ 12.3 Å × 12.3 Å), in which the atoms of marginal sites in the supercell are removed. Each GQD is separated by a vacuum layer of ∼ 15 Å in the surface normal to minimize the artificial interactions due to periodic boundary condition employed in simulations. A GOQD is simulated by decorating O atoms at the edge sites of the GQD, and the effects of boron-doping are investigated by partially replacing C with B atoms, or B decoration at the edge sites of GOQDs to get BGOQDs.

Figs. 1(a) and 1(b) show the TEM images of GOQDs and BGOQDs360, respectively. The size distributions for GOQDs and BGOQDs360 are depicted in Figs. 1(c) and 1(d). The diameters of GOQDs and BGOQDs360 range from approximately 0.9 to 2.2 nm and 1.40–2.90 nm, respectively. The average diameters were calculated to be around 1.38 nm for GOQDs and 1.99 nm for BGOQDs360.

Raman spectroscopy is widely recognized as an effective tool for investigating the molecular structure of carbon-based materials. Fig. 2(a) shows the Raman spectra of GOQDs and BGOQDs. The D and G peaks of GOQDs appear at 1390 cm⁻¹ and 1616 cm⁻¹ , respectively. Notably, in BGOQDs, the D peak is blue-shifted by 6 cm⁻¹ to 1396 cm⁻¹ , while the G peak is red-shifted by 9 cm⁻¹ to 1607 cm⁻¹ , compared to GOQDs. This shift may be attributed to structural distortion in BGOQDs caused by the differing bond lengths of C-C and B-C, as well as the different atomic masses of carbon and boron [28]. These findings suggest a change in lattice vibrations due to the introduction of boron impurities or dopants. Additionally, the intensity ratios (ID/IG) of the D peak (ID) to the G peak (IG) for BGOQDs300, BGOQDs320, BGOQDs340, and BGOQDs360 were approximately 0.97, 1.00, 0.98, and 0.98, respectively, larger than that of GOQDs (0.93), which indicates that the BGOQDs possess a higher disorder of arrangement than GOQDs.

FT-IR spectroscopy was performed to further characterize and identify the surface functional groups presented in the GOQDs and BGOQDs. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the figure clearly reveals broad absorption bands in the range of 3300–3700 cm⁻¹ for both GOQDs and BGOQDs, which can be attributed to the characteristic -OH stretching vibrations [29]. Furthermore, the prominent bands at 1719 cm⁻¹ and 1246 cm⁻¹ correspond to CO and C-C/C-O stretching modes, respectively. It is observed that the -COOH vibrational absorption band at 1447 cm⁻¹ for GOQDs nearly disappears. The CC vibrational absorption band at 1617 cm⁻¹ can be seen both in GOQDs and BGOQDs. Notably, all BGOQDs exhibit new bands at 1041 cm⁻¹ , 1112 cm⁻¹ , and 1390 cm⁻¹ , which can be attributed to B-O-H, B-C [30] and O-B [31,32] stretching, respectively.

XPS was employed to characterize the chemical composition of the samples. Fig. 3(a) shows that the pristine GOQDs contain two major elements, C1s (about 284.4 eV) and O1s (about 531.4 eV). In contrast, additional B1s peaks, located around 191.4 eV (Fig. 3b) [33], are detected in all BGOQDs. The relative content of each element in GOQDs and BGOQDs varies significantly, as shown in Table 1. Notably, the relative content of C increases progressively with rising annealing temperature, while the oxygen (O) content shows an opposite trend. The B contents of BGOQDs300, BGOQDs320, BGOQDs340, and BGOQDs360 are found to be 0.31, 1.00, 1.08, and 0.95 at%, respectively. This indicates that the B content in BGOQDs can be tuned by adjusting the annealing temperature. Additionally, it is evident that the relative O content decreases as the B doping level increases. We speculate that the O functional groups on GOQDs are removed with the release of CO₂ and CO during thermal annealing process [34,35], during which B atoms are incorporated into the lattice of GOQDs.

Fig. 3(c) presents the high-resolution C1s spectra of GOQDs and BGOQDs. The CC peak is observed at 284.2 eV. Additionally, the peaks at 288.52, 285.99, 287.88, and 284.85 eV correspond to O-CO, C-O-C, CO, and C-C, respectively. While the peaks at 283.55 and 289.85 eV are attributed to B-C and C-O-B bonds, respectively. Fig. 3(d) shows the high-resolution B1s spectra of BGOQDs. The high-resolution O1s spectra for GOQDs and BGOQDs are displayed in Fig.S1. The high-resolution B1s spectra can be deconvoluted into four peaks corresponding to BC₃ (189.08 eV), BC₂O (189.99 eV), BCO₂ (190.75 eV), and B₂O₃ (191.85 eV). Moreover, with the annealing temperature increasing from 300 to 360 ℃, the peaks to some chemical bonding induces a shift. This shift may be attributed to electron donation of heteroatom to C-atom, which leads to larger electron cloud density on C-atoms [36]. These findings clearly indicate that B atoms were successfully doped into the lattice of GOQDs.

The C1s spectra were subsequently analyzed quantitatively, and Table 2 outlines the chemical group contents of GOQDs and BGOQDs. Compared to GOQDs, the BGOQDs exhibit a slight increase in the contents of C-O-C bonds and CC bonds, while the CO and O-CO bonds are significantly reduced. This suggests that most of the oxygen-containing functional groups are pyrolyzed [37] or converted into heat-stabilized phenolic hydroxyls, ethers, and carbonyls [38]. However, the CC bond content in BGOQDs decreased from 43.51 at% in GOQDs to 39.78, 32.09, 29.24, and 27.04 at% in BGOQDs300, BGOQDs320, BGOQDs340, and BGOQDs360, respectively, while the C-B bonds increased from 0 at% to 4.14, 5.67, 11.32, and 15.56 at% in BGOQDs300, BGOQDs320, BGOQDs340, and BGOQDs360, respectively. This indicates that the breaking of CC bonds in GOQDs facilitated the formation of B-C bonds between the B atoms in boron oxide and the carbon atoms in GOQDs. We hypothesize that the introduction of boron during the thermal annealing process reduces the CC bonds in the various samples, while the high temperature converts C-C to CC, which subsequently breaks to form B-C bonds. Additionally, the fluctuations in the content of C-O-C bonds are accompanied by inversely correlated fluctuations in C-O-B bonds. This could be attributed to the fact that C-O-C in the sample represents epoxy and ether groups at the edges, while the surface epoxy groups are unstable and prone to breaking during annealing [38], leading to the formation of C-O-B bonds with B atoms.

PL spectra were measured to gain a deeper understanding of the optical properties of BGOQDs. As is well known, fluorescent carbon materials typically exhibit excitation-dependent PL behavior [39–41]. Fig. 4a-e illustrate the PL behavior of GOQDs and BGOQDs. GOQDs display excitation-independent PL behavior when the excitation wavelength is varied from 330 nm to 450 nm, with the emission peak consistently located at 525.5 nm (Fig. 4a). In contrast, BGOQDs exhibit different PL behaviors under the same excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4b-e). The PL emission peaks of BGOQDs300, BGOQDs320, BGOQDs340, and BGOQDs360 were located at 507.8, 504.3, 495.3, and 490.0 nm, respectively.

The PL spectra of GOQDs and BGOQDs under 360 nm irradiation ( Fig. 5a) were normalized, respectively. It is evident that the PL emission peaks of BGOQDs are blue-shifted compared to that of GOQDs. In Fig. 5(c), the black curve illustrates the dependence of the PL blue-shift and the annealing temperature in BGOQDs, which increases with increasing annealing temperature, reaching a maximal blue-shift of 35.5 nm for BGOQDs360. Given that the C-B content in BGOQDs also increases with increasing annealing temperature (as shown in Table 2), it is reasonable to infer that the PL blue-shift in BGOQDs may be related to the presence of C-B bonds. The relationship between the level of PL blue-shift and the B-C content in BGOQDs was analyzed. As depicted in Fig. 5(c), one can clearly see that the PL blue-shift follows the same trend as the increase in C-B content. This indicates that a higher B-C content leads to a greater PL blue-shift in BGOQDs. These findings suggest that the presence of B-C bonds is likely responsible for the PL blue-shift observed in BGOQDs.

It is well known that quantum confinement effects can cause a blue-shift in the PL emission peaks of GQDs when their size is reduced [42]. However, a comparison of the sizes of BGOQDs360 and GOQDs in this study shows an increase in the size of BGOQDs360 (Fig. 1a-d). This indicates that the blue-shift observed in BGOQDs may be not primarily due to quantum confinement effects.

The optical properties of each sample were characterized by UV–visible absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. 5(b), both GOQDs and BGOQDs exhibit a absorption peak around 300 nm. Furthermore, leveraging the UV-Vis absorption spectra, we determined the band gap energy of our samples. The band gap energy of the samples was calculated using the Kubelka-Munk method based on the UV-Vis absorption spectra. Specifically, the following equation was employed:
(1) (αhv)2=A(hv-Eg)

where α, h, and ν represent the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively [43–45]. Using this relationship, we plotted (αhv)2 versus hv, as shown in Fig. S2. It was observed that the introduction of B atoms leads to an upward shift in the energy levels, which is confirmed by the increase in band gap energy. The specific band gap energies for GOQDs, BGOQDs300, BGOQDs320, BGOQDs340, and BGOQDs360 are 4.74, 4.79, 4.80, 4.84, and 5.09 eV, respectively. This shift also causes a blue-shift in the PL emission peaks. These findings are consistent with the results obtained from the PL spectra, further validating the influence of B atoms on the electronic structure and optical properties of the samples. Fig. 5(d) shows the optical images of GOQDs and BGOQDs under UV lamp irradiation at a wavelength of 365 nm. GOQDs display a bright yellow luminescence, while BGOQDs exhibit different luminescent colors compared to GOQDs. Specifically, the luminescent colors of BGOQDs300, BGOQDs320, BGOQDs340, and BGOQDs360 are greenish-yellow, aqua green, lime green, and cyan, respectively. These results demonstrate that boron doping can effectively modulate the optical properties of GOQDs.

From the experimental results discussed above, it is clear that the microscopic mechanism of PL in BGOQDs is complex and requires further investigation. To better understand the physical mechanism behind the PL blue-shift in BGOQDs, DFT calculations were performed on a series of BGOQDs with varying numbers of B-C bonds. The original GOQD, together with two typical BGOQD configurations are shown in Figs. 6(a) to 6(c). For simplicity, the configuration with three B-C bonds is labeled as configuration A (cfg. A), and the one with four B-C bonds is labeled as configuration B (cfg. B). As seen in Figs. 6(b) and 6(c), depending on the doping geometries, five-, six-, or seven-member atomic rings can form in the BGOQDs.

PL is essentially the inverse process of optical absorption, which can be described by the imaginary part of the dielectric function (conventionally denoted as ε2). The calculated imaginary part of the dielectric functions for the simulated GOQD and BGOQDs (configurations A and B) are shown in Fig. 6(d) as a function of photon energy. It is evident that the optical spectra exhibit a general blue-shift trend as the number of B-C bonds increases from zero (GOQD) to three and to four. For instance, the first peak (from the low-energy side) of GOQD sitting at hv ∼ 0.405 eV (λ ∼ 3069.3 nm) is blue-shifted to hv ∼ 0.668 eV (λ ∼ 1860.9 nm) in configuration A and hv ∼ 1.071 eV (λ ∼ 1160.7 nm) in configuration B, respectively. Additionally, from configuration A to B, the peaks located at approximately hv ∼ 0.668 eV (λ ∼ 1860.9 nm), 1.001 eV (λ ∼ 1241.9 nm), and 1.901 eV (λ ∼ 653.9 nm) are blue-shifted to hv ∼ 1.071 eV (λ ∼ 1160.7 nm), 1.415 eV (λ ∼ 878.5 nm), and 2.130 eV (λ ∼ 583.6 nm), respectively.

The blue-shift due to the variation of B-C bonds in the BGOQDs can be understood as follows: An increase of B-C bonds introduces holes to the electron bonding states especially the state corresponding to the valence band maximum (VBM), which lowers the position of VBM and enlarges the band gap. To support this, we analyzed the energy levels and wave functions of the VBM and conduction bands (CB) related to the two peaks around ∼ 2 eV for configurations A and B, focusing on the Γ point, which significantly contributes to the absorption/emission spectra. The results are shown in Fig. 7. Compared to configuration A, an enlarged band gap (Eg,Γ ∼ 2.135 eV) is found in configuration B, due to the downward shift of VBM and the upward shift of CB, resulting in blue-shift in the optical absorption/emission spectra. By examining the spatial distributions of the electron densities of VBM and CB (i.e.,
ψ
Γ
VBM
2
and
ψ
Γ
CB
2
) shown in Fig. 7, it is clear that the wave functions of configuration B are distributed over more atoms than in configuration A, suggesting stronger orbital hybridization and consequently enlarged band gaps [46].

In summary, we successfully prepared BGOQDs with tunable PL through a simple thermal annealing method, using GOQDs powder as the precursor and B₂O₃ as the doping source. The PL emission of BGOQDs exhibited a maximum blue-shift of 35.5 nm. The PL of BGOQDs can be adjusted from yellow to cyan by regulating the annealing temperature. DFT calculations demonstrate that as the increase of B-C bonds in the BGOQDs lowers the position of VBM and enlarges the band gap and then leads to a PL blue-shift in BGOQDs. Our results open up a way to prepare BGOQDs with cyan emission and contribute to the understanding of the atomic mechanism of color tunability of BGOQDs. These results provide valuable technical support for the application of GOQDs in the field of cyan light.

Kong Wenjie: Project administration, Formal analysis. Liu Jun: Validation, Supervision. Zhu Changming: Supervision, Methodology. Lv Fengzhen: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wei Xuelin: Validation, Investigation. Ouyang Hong: Validation, Investigation. Luo Hongyi: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Kawazoe Yoshiyuki: Software. Cheng Longxu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhang Qiwei: Validation, Supervision. Zhang Mao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Yang Yong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation. Liu Fuchi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region (No.2021GXNSFAA220044), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12164005, 11664003, 11474285, 12104106) P. R. China. We thank the staff of the Center for Computational Materials Science, Institute for Materials Research, Tohoku University, and the supercomputer resources through the HPCI System Research Project (hp200246). The authors also appreciate the staff of Hefei Advanced Computing Center for the support of supercomputing facilities. One of the authors (Y. Kawazoe), is supported by Suranaree University of Technology (SUT), Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI), and National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF).

ce:section.ce:para: The supplementary material provides the high-resolution O1s spectra for GOQDs and BGOQDs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2025.178782.
